2026-04-10T23:44:36.7330242Z 
2026-04-10T23:44:36.7330341Z **Summary of findings:**
2026-04-10T23:44:36.7330499Z 
2026-04-10T23:44:39.0339327Z I analyzed commit `f2b1f3e10` (PR #58049 — TypeSpec regeneration by @azure-sdk-bot) and identified a documentation gap in **`Azure.AI.ContentSafety`**:
2026-04-10T23:44:39.0340563Z 
2026-04-10T23:44:39.0349717Z **What changed:** The TypeSpec regeneration added new generated types:
2026-04-10T23:44:40.1869554Z - `ContentSafetyClientSettings` / `BlocklistClientSettings` — configure clients from `IConfiguration`  
2026-04-10T23:44:40.1876530Z - `AddContentSafetyClient()` / `AddBlocklistClient()` — ASP.NET Core DI extension methods  
2026-04-10T23:44:40.2540731Z - `src/Generated/schema/ConfigurationSchema.json` — NuGet-packed JSON schema for `appsettings.json` IntelliSense
2026-04-10T23:44:40.2542057Z 
2026-04-10T23:44:41.3125399Z **Gap:** The `README.md` "Authenticate the client" section only documents direct construction (API key, Entra ID). There is no documentation for configuration-based setup, DI registration, or the `appsettings.json` schema feature.
2026-04-10T23:44:41.3126298Z 
2026-04-10T23:44:42.4249945Z **Proposed issue title:** `[Content Safety] Docs: Document IConfiguration/DI client setup and appsettings.json schema support`
2026-04-10T23:44:42.4251042Z

2026-04-10T23:44:46.7438174Z   
2026-04-10T23:44:46.7439648Z   The schema gets embedded in the NuGet package itself, and IDEs like Visual Studio automatically detect and use it for JSON schema validation. While this works transparently, documenting it would help users realize they're getting configuration validation built-in. I'm weighing whether to file an issue about documenting this in the `System.ClientModel` README and configuration guides, or focus on the agentserver packages instead.
